title: Lesson 418
transcript: OK, I wanted to add one little video here to give you a good example of a question on what we just talked about, the whole reference monitor concept, where the whole idea of a reference monitor concept, it's all about a subject wanting to access an object. And if we want to have security, we can't just let any subject access any object and do whatever they want. We have to have some form of mediation in between them. And that mediation has to be based on a set of rules. And we need to log and monitor all of this. That's the reference monitor concept. And of course, the implementation of the RMC is always known as a security kernel. And here's the three properties. And all this is contained within the TCB, along with many other things. We just covered this, right? Security kernel, RMC, TCB. You might get questions on these and they're all very similar. So I have a good question here that will help you, I think, understand the difference between these and how you would answer a question on the exam about these. So here's the simple question. A user logs into their laptop by entering their username and password. Is this the TCB, the reference monitor concept, or the security kernel? Now, I've been asking this question in our live classes for many years now. And I can tell you with experience that on any good sized class, because our classes are usually 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 people in any class that size, you're going to get lots of people all saying, oh, that's the TCB, the trusted computing base. A bunch more people will say, oh, the answer to here, the best answer here is the reference monitor concept. And a bunch more people will say it's the security kernel. So if you've answered any one of those, don't feel bad. Someone, everyone always thinks it's these three things. And here's why this is a really good question. And this is a good example of an exam question. All of these are correct to a degree, right? A user logging into their laptop, like let's, so let's go through them, right? A user logging into their laptop by entering their username and password. Is this correct? The TCB. And the answer is to a degree, yes, right? A user logging into their laptop, that indicates that some sort of authentication mechanism has been put in place. An authentication mechanism is obviously a security control that would be contained within the trusted computing base. Because remember, the TCB is the totality control. of protection mechanisms and so this is a security control that would be contained within the tcb so the tcb is not an incorrect answer but just like many questions you'll see on the exam these are all correct answers to a degree so you're forced to pick which is the best answer And the TCB is not the best answer here. Why? Well, you probably picked out the keywords of what I just said a second ago. This is a security control. The perfect definition of the TCB is it's the totality, the collection of all security mechanisms. This is just one security mechanism. It's not all of them. So TCB is not the best answer, right? Because this is just one security mechanism. It's not all of the security mechanisms, which is the TCB. Okay, so we're down to RMC and security kernel. So a user logging into their laptop by entering their username and password, that's a subject accessing an object through some form of mediation. So this is absolutely based on the reference monitor concept. That's a correct answer. And of course, this is also a security kernel. So now we're forced to choose between RMC and security kernel. They're both correct to a degree. So which is the best answer here? Security kernel. Why? Well, a user logging into their laptop, that indicates something has been implemented, right? So if you're choosing between the RMC and the security kernel, always think about, has something been implemented? If the answer is yes, something has been implemented, then it's the security kernel. If it's just talking about a concept, that would be the RMC. So for example, if this text was slightly different, let's just hide here. Let's say the question said, what is the notion of a user authenticating to their laptop? What is the notion? Notion implies what is the idea? What's the concept of a user authenticating? Then the perfect answer to that question would be the reference monitor concept. But because this question implies, right? A user is actually typing in their username and password to log in. Something has been implemented. The best answer here is the security kernel. So I hope that helps you think about these terms. And this is also kind of some good exam strategy that you should be. This is the sort of things you should be thinking through when you're doing the exam. think about what's the perfect definition of each of these and therefore which of you know this is just a sec this is a security mechanism that's being implemented which best fits the definitions here that's the type of thing you have to do in the exam so hopefully that helps
